comments.author: @HorizonBoulevard
comments.text:
Just a clarification! The Country Nobody Wanted isn't about Slovak people or identity, it's about how independence happened. In 1992, most Czechs and Slovaks actually wanted to stay together, but two politicians split the country anyway, no referendum, no vote. That's the nobody wanted part. It's not a comment on Slovak nationhood, identity, or history... the whole video is a tribute to that. 

We think Slovakia is a really beautiful country with an incredible story, and wanted to share it with more people. Thanks so much for watching!

comments.author: @ViktorNovak-y3i
comments.text: It is not true that the Slovenes/Slovaks had their first independent state. The Principality of Nitra was a significant power structure that was formed at the end of the 8th century in the territory of present-day Slovakia. Its center was Nitra. It played a key role in Slovak history and laid the foundations for the emergence of the first common state of the ancient Slovaks. Period of independence: The most significant and at the same time the last known independent prince was Pribina. In 828, he had the first known Christian church consecrated in Nitra on Slovak territory. The emergence of Great Moravia (833): The Moravian prince Mojmír I invaded the Nitra region, expelled Pribina and, by uniting both territories, created Great Moravia. Nitra maintained its position as the second most important center and principality in it. After being annexed to Great Moravia and later in the Kingdom of Hungary (as Tercia pars regni – a third of the kingdom), the territorial extent shifted. It included not only the whole of present-day Slovakia, but also parts of present-day northern Hungary (approximately up to the Tisza River), southern Poland and eastern Moravia. Incidentally, the name Slovak, Slovaks in the masculine gender was introduced only in the Middle Ages at the beginning of Magyarization. The correct name is Sloven, Sloveni, which has been preserved in other grammatical forms to this day - Sloven-ka, Sloven-sko, sloven-ský/í.

comments.author: @963ag
comments.text: Both of my parents were Hungarian refugees... My mother's family descended from nobility - the Tisza Ujhelyi-Ujhelyi family. I have been doing extensive genealogical research, as the family tree is well recorded, and I noticed that many of the surnames of my female ancestors  are Slovak - the males are all of the Hungarian Ujhelyi line, but my great and great -great grandmothers' maiden names were Lehotszky and Szulyovszky, ( I know that the " sky" ending is Slavic, and was changed to " szky" the Hungarian form) I am very interested in history, so I am learning more about Slovakia, now.

replies.author: @ViktorNovak-y3i
replies.text: Because, Original Population: Modern Hungarians are genetically almost identical to surrounding peoples and are primarily descendants of the original population of the Carpathian Basin (including Slavs and other earlier cultures) who were Magyarized in the Middle Ages. Original Old Hungarians: The genetic heritage of the original old (nomadic) Hungarians constitutes only a fraction of the present-day population, estimated at around 5%. DNA Composition: The rest of the gene pool is a mix of Central European, Slavic, Germanic, Romance and other genes that have been mixed in the region for millennia. Genetics clearly separates language from biology. Although Hungarians speak a Finno-Ugric language, from a biological point of view they are largely Central European.

comments.author: @dudogersak
comments.text: A wonderful documentary. But you have a fundamental error there. Mining of mineral resources was in mountains such as the Kremnické vrchy, which are of volcanic origin, and the Low Tatras, which are limestone. ... On the contrary, the High Tatras are granite and gneiss, which are some of the hardest rocks, which is why they are also sharp, steep and little weathered. Which is unique compared to other weathered mountain ranges in the whole of Europe, including the Alps.

comments.author: @ancientslav4863
comments.text:
I live in Slovakia for over 36 years. Aside our northern Tatras and surrounding areas, its mostly pretty only from afar. Nature cab be pretty, but stupid people, and there are plenty, throw garbage out of cars daily, piles of trash everywhere, even fields. Ive seen agriculture tractors just mow it all down into the ground, regular trash bags filled with all of the shit you can find in garbage dumps, all into fields. Dirty garbage hobo filled streets, especially Bratislava, the capitol city. 

Huge gypsie problems, mafias, corrupt politicians, really bad economy and people still living on nothing, paycheck to paycheck, meaningless bad jobs mostly. Most seniors are poor as mice, can barely afford groceries. Now we have also Ukrainian "refugee" problems. Not all of them are bad, but plenty bad ones came. Schooling isn't much either, and plenty of neck tattoo wannabe gangsters who act like the country belongs to them. Daily issues of immorality and people who would do anything but help another. Not all is bad, but Slovakia is slowly falling into stupidity and immorality and virtue less behavior. 

Country is stagnant, has issues left and right.

comments.author: @mixi1141
comments.text: Slovensko je nadherna krajina, ale slovaci si nezasluzia svoj samostatny stat. Nevedia si ho sami spravovat. A to je smutne. 30 rokov po porazke komunizmu a kam sme to dotiahli... Politici kradnu miliony, obyvatelia su rozostvani jeden proti druhemu a ziadna vidina zlepsenia dalsie desatrocia, pretoze politicka reprezentacia su bud zlodeji, podvodnici, alebo blazni, ktori sa striedaju vo vlade.

replies.author: @TheFedor47
replies.text:
​@mixi1141 Máme viac diaľníc a rýchlostných ciest než Poliaci aj v prepočte na obyvateľa aj v prepočte na rozlohu krajiny.
Zároveň my poväčšine staviame v náročnom teréne a Poliaci na nížinatej rovine, to je veľký rozdiel.
Nikto im to neberie budujú rýchlo a ekonomicky im to tiež ide dobre, lebo na rozdiel od nás majú lepšie diverzifikovanú ekonomiku a majú veľký vnútorný trh, ktorý ich cez krízu podrží, my máme malý trh a sme zameraní na export. My zasa máme jadrové elektrárne a oni idú na uhlie.
Netvrdím, že sa nedá budovať rýchlejšie ani že ekonomicky ide všetko dobre.
Dá sa veľa vecí zmeniť k lepšiemu, aby naša ekonomika nebola taká labilná a aby nám absolventi neodchádzali do zahraničia. (Paradoxne veľa doplácame na príbuznosť s Čechmi, keďže sú bohatší a jazyková bariéra je minimálna)
Na druhú stranu je tu veľa dobrých vecí, ktoré sú dôležitejšie ako peniaze.
Mám kamaráta z bohatého Švédska ktorý býva dodnes na Slovensku, založil si tu rodinu a povedal, že sa v živote necítil tak bezpečne, keďže v Štokholme mu išlo o krk na dennom poriadku. Predtým býval aj v Írsku ani tam nechcel zostať.
Je XYZ vecí ktoré sa dajú vymenovať a sú na Slovensku dobré aj keď nie všetko je ideálne. Preto tvrdiť, že si nezaslúžime štát, len preto, že niekomu sa darí lepšie je scestné.
Sú aj krajiny ktorým sa darí horšie a čo teraz? Nezaslúžia si svoj štát?
A pod kým nám teda bolo dobre? Pod Maďarmi? Tam to bolo parádne?

replies.author: @mixi1141
replies.text: ​@TheFedor47 ja si myslim, ze 37 rokov je dost na to, aby sme sa naucili riadit si vlastny stat. Ale nam to zjavne nestaci. Za 37 rokov sme neboli schopni anilen postavit jednu dialnicu zo zapadu na vychod.  Vlada sa chvali najnizsim dlhom, ale obycajnemu cloveku je to jedno. Lebo vie, ze clovek v nemecku ma 3x taky plat ako tu, dochodca 3x vyssi dochodok. V Polsku je priemerny plat o 500 eur vyssi ako u nas. Polsko ma vystavane dialnice krizom krazom. Pritom aj Nemecko aj Polsko maju dlh vyssi ako Slovensko. Aj v Cesku je zivotna uroven o poznanie vyssia ako u nas. Pokial ale ide o politikov a clenov vlady, ti su na tom v porovnani s inymi krajinami velmi dobre. Platy si pozvysovali tak, ze ich maju vyssie ako inde. Nadelili si renty a kradnu ako straky.  Niekde je teda chyba.

replies.author: @TheFedor47
replies.text:
Takéto plakanie mám najradšej.
Iné krajiny prechádzali tým čím prechádzame my stovky rokov dozadu, sme mladá krajina a prechádzame si svojím vývojom. Kdesi od Veľkej Moravy do 1993 nás v podstate vždy niekto viac či menej riadil. Aj to sa treba naučiť a vybudovať si svoju inteligenciu ktorá bola dlhodobo na našom území zanedbávaná. Sú dobré a sú zlé veci, avšak máme byť na čo hrdí.
Máme mnoho šikovných a talentovaných ľudí, odborníkov v rôznych odboroch.
Sme jednou z najpriemyselnejších krajín, sme dobrí v produkcii čistej elektriny, máme dobrú aj keď nie úžasnú infraštruktúru, aj pre nás samozrejmé veci ako nejaký rozhlas alebo to, že autobusy jazdia pomaly všade nie je v niektorých okolitých krajinách úplne samozrejmé, tých vecí je mnoho.
Komentár, že si nezaslúžime ani len samostatný štát len ďalej podkopáva naše národné sebavedomie. To je to posledné čo potrebujeme.

comments.author: @emaspork4260
comments.text: What kind of nonsense is this? To say that Slovakia suddenly appeared and that nobody wanted it is simply false. Slovakia had existed for many years before it became fully independent in the 1990s, and it happened because the Slovaks wanted it. As early as the 15th and 16th centuries, the name Slovakia (Latin: Slovakia, Sclavonia, and similar forms) appeared as a geographical designation for the territory inhabited by Slovaks - with their own Slovak nation, Slovak culture, and Slovak language. So stop spreading nonsense!

comments.author: @AxisCapricorn
comments.text:
The mining of precious and non-ferrous metals in the territory of present-day Slovakia was of global importance in the Middle Ages and early modern times. Slovakia was not only a local producer, but also the economic heart of Hungary and one of the most important pillars of the European economy. The importance of mining is best illustrated by three famous Slovak mining towns, each of which dominated a different metal:
Kremnica – “Golden City”
Banská Štiavnica – “Silver City”
Banská Bystrica – “Copper City”
Thanks to this immense concentration of mineral wealth and the influx of German experts (Saxon colonists), Slovak mining towns had exceptional royal privileges (Free Royal Mining Towns). Castles in Slovakia were built to protect this unimaginable wealth that was mined from its underground.

In the 14th century, huge amounts of gold were mined in Slovakia – according to estimates, it was up to a third of the global and most of the European production. In 1328, a mint was founded in Kremnica by King Charles I Robert of Anjou. The Kremnica Ducats had the status of “the dollar of the Middle Ages” due to the exceptionally high purity of the gold. They were the most sought-after, most stable and most reliable currency in all of Europe. Ducats were minted in medieval Kremnica during the Angevin period on the Hungarian throne from gold of 23 carats and 9 grains (purity 0.990). They were exact copies of the gold coins of the city of Florence called florins. The Latin inscription on a similar gold Venetian coin ended with the words "iste ducatus", which is how this type of coin was named Ducat. All Kremnica ducats were distinguished by a high and constant gold purity, one grain higher (0.003) than all other coins of this type in contemporary Europe (0.986), thanks to which they were considered the hardest currency in Europe in the Middle Ages. The mint in Kremnica has been operating continuously since its founding to this day, which makes it one of the oldest enterprises in the world. It is the oldest mint. The city castle in Kremnica protected this wealth. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=gSgqVSf0zd0&t=1s

Banská Štiavnica was the most important producer of silver in Europe from the 14th to the 18th century. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7X8c1rWLxYM This metal determined the direction of world science, technology and education. In 1627, gunpowder was used for peaceful (mining) purposes for the first time in the world in the local Bieber mine. This invention started a revolution in world mining. In 1762, Maria Theresa founded the world's first technical university, the Mining Academy (Bergademia). Štiavnica became a world center of science and research in the fields of metallurgy, chemistry and mining.

Copper ores in Španá Dolina and Staré Hory showed extraordinary wealth. At the turn of the 15th and 16th centuries, thanks to the capital of Jakob Fugger and the engineering of Ján Thurzo, central Slovakia became the largest copper producer in the world. Slovak copper became a global commodity. It was exported to Antwerp, Lisbon and Venice. It was used to cover the roofs of European cathedrals, produce works for defense against the Turks and was a key commodity loaded on the ships of Portuguese and Spanish explorers on their voyages to India, China and America. The Thurzo-Fugger company controlled almost 40% of the European copper market and built the largest mining and metallurgical complex in the world (Medený hámor) in Banská Bystrica. In the Little Carpathians, located just a few dozen kilometers from Bratislava, is one of the best-preserved Slovak castles, Červený Kameň. https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0e_cf9qwFPY It has colossal underground storage facilities. In the 16th century, it was rebuilt into a massive fortress with a fortification system that was one of the pinnacles of the military-defense strategy of the time. The castle served as a fortified transshipment point and central warehouse for copper extracted in the Banská Bystrica mines, which were started decades ago by Jakob Fugger.

The Thurzo-Fugger Company (officially Ungarischer Kupferhandel – Hungarian Copper Trade) was established by a founding agreement on March 16, 1495 in Banská Bystrica, in German Neusohl. At the turn of the 15th and 16th centuries, it was the largest and most modern corporation of its kind in the world, which effectively heralded the era of modern global capitalism. It was a fusion of top-notch Slovak technical know-how and unlimited German capital. Ján Thurzo (from Spiš): A brilliant mining engineer and metallurgist. He invented a new technology for cleaning ores and draining flooded mines. Jakob Fugger (from Augsburg): A financial magnate. He provided huge financial resources for buying up mines and building infrastructure. Fugger dominated the European copper and silver market. This gave him financial power that no monarch of the time could compete with. Jakob Fugger (known as "The Rich") was a German merchant, mining entrepreneur, and banker who lived from 1459 to 1525. He is considered one of the richest men in human history. His enormous influence shaped the history of early modern Europe, financing kings, emperors, and popes. Jakob Fugger's relationship to the sale of indulgences and the subsequent Reformation of Martin Luther is one of the most fascinating chapters in world history. Jakob Fugger was the main financial engine behind the entire indulgence scandal that led to the collapse of Western Christianity.

comments.author: @dusanputnik
comments.text:
There is a saying in Slovakia - not all that glitters is gold.

The country is actually not free, it is swallowed up by the Fourth BruSSels Reich.
Yes, even before that Czechoslovakia was part of the Soviet bloc, but with all the necessary economy without unemployment, with developed light and heavy industry and full self-sufficient agriculture, with production from pins to shoemaking and textile industries to machines, turbines and locomotives.

Free education and healthcare for everyone, cheap rent and stable prices. Czechoslovakia was among the most advanced economies in the world before and after WW2, during the monarchy, also its most developed parts.

The BruSSels Fourth Reich destroyed all of that, broke up the original republic - as before prosperous and flourishing monarchy.

Poverty has arisen and is dramatically increasing, hundreds of thousands Gypsies live in ghettos without work for generations - money for building for all, will be stolen by the political and economic mafia, well-paid lackeys of BruSSels.

So as the video shows, the only beautiful things left here is nature and old buildings. What the Western wars did not destroy before, the fascist corporate BruSSels destroyed later.

replies.author: @dusanputnik
replies.text:
Stefan Zweig
The World of Yesterday.

Perhaps nothing more graphically illustrates the monstrous relapse the world suffered after the First World War than the restrictions on personal freedom of movement and civil rights.

Before 1914 the earth belonged to the entire human race.  Everyone could go where he wanted and stay there as long as he liked.  No permits or visas were necessary, and I am always enchanted by the amazement of young people when I tell them that before 1914 I travelled to India and America without a passport.

Indeed, I had never set eyes on a passport.  You boarded your means of transport and got off it again, without asking or being asked any questions; you didn’t have to fill in a single one of the hundred forms required today.

Ako by sa v takej vypätej a klamnej atmosfére konalo referendum a ako by dopadlo? Ani ČSR po rozpade Rakusko-Uhorska r.1918 nevznikla na základe referenda. Kto upiera slovákom nárok na sebeurčenie a štátnosť mal by si prečítať základné články charty OSN kde je ustanovené právo národom na sebeurčenie. Ako bola naplnená či lepšie povedané nenaplnená táto dohoda poznáme z histórie. Spôsob rozdelenia bol správny (nenásilný, kulturny a kultivovaný) - to je fakt a nie nejakých vycucaných 10% z prsta a čechoslovakistické rozprávky o Mečiarovi. Rozdiely slovákov a čechov v mentalite, ekonomike nedávali ČSFR dlhú životnosť, buddťe radi že je to ako je, teraz by sme sa v slopočnom štáte nenávideli(viď Juhoslávia).Správajte sa už konečne sebavedomo, dospelo a nesnažte sa vyhovieť len druhým k ich prospechu, myslite aj na svoj (slovenský)prospech a záujmy.

replies.text: ​​@prde66V tom nadpise sa píše, že Slovensko je "krajina", ktorú "nikto" nechcel, nie "štát", ktorý nikto nechcel a v oboch prípadoch to nie je pravda, keďže veľa z nás, ktorí milujeme svoju Vlasť, sme Slovensko určite chceli a to ako krajinu, tak aj štát. Takže si tieto svoje nostalgické a čechoslovakistické drísty o desiatich percentách nechajte pre seba a pre niekoho, kto je vám podobný! 🤦😁
